attributes.stem:
A 55-year-old man presents with a three month history of pain in his right calf when he walks more than 100 metres. The pain ceases when he rests. He is overweight and has a 30 pack-year smoking history.

On examination his blood pressure is 150/100 mmHg and his pulse rate is 90/min. Both lower limbs and feet are warm and well perfused. The right femoral pulse is diminished in volume and the right pedal pulse cannot be felt. His ankle-brachial index is 1.0 on the left and 0.8 on the right.

The patient is given advice on dietary adjustment and smoking cessation.
attributes.stemQuestion: In addition to this advice, which one of the following is the most appropriate initial management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient's history and examination are characteristic of intermittent claudication arising from peripheral arterial disease (PAD), which is confirmed by the correspondingly reduced ankle-brachial index (ABI) in the right lower extremity. With his non-disabling claudication and ABI of 0.8, this patient has 'mild' PAD.

In this setting, the overlapping triad of risk factor modification (e.g. smoking cessation, glycaemic control, hypertension reduction), pharmacological treatment (aspirin and statin plus/minus antihypertensive), and a formal graded exercise program is associated with improved walking distance and reduction in risk of progression, amputation, other cardiovascular events, and death. Imaging with either duplex ultrasonography or CT angiography will not likely change management and are not necessary at this stage.

An exercise program for claudication generally entails:

  • 30 minutes of brisk exercise per day

  • Walking until moderate pain is felt, resting until it subsides, and restarting

  • Aiming to reach pain threshold within 5-7 minutes

These cycles of rest and exercise are thought to improve oxygen extraction by the muscles and improve claudication symptoms through this and multiple other mechanisms.

Clopidogrel has been shown to have similar efficacy to aspirin in PAD (in the CAPRIE trial), however is not PBS-subsidised for this indication in Australia and therefore entails excess cost to the patient and as such is not first-line therapy.

Though this patient likely does require antihypertensive therapy, a beta-blocker (e.g. atenolol) is also not first-line; in PAD, ACE inhibitors or angiotensin receptor blockers are preferred due to their additional cardioprotective effects (as shown in the HOPE and ONTARGET trials).
learningPoints: Exercise programs improve both pain-free and maximum walking distance in intermittent claudication.
Exercise programs are a cornerstone of the treatment of mild peripheral arterial disease.
references: Exercise for intermittent claudication. Lane R, Harwood A, Watson L, Leng GC. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2017 Dec 26;12(12):CD000990.
Medical management of claudication. Ratchford EV. J Vasc Surg. 2017 Jul;66(1):275-280.
